entries.description: Co-hosts Canada qualified for the men's World Cup knock-out stages for the first time but lost out on top spot and a Vancouver last-32 game to Switzerland after a 2-1 defeat on Wednesday. Second-half goals from Ruben Vargas and Johan Manzambi meant the Swiss finished top of Group B on seven points and the Canadians ended up in second spot on four, above Bosnia and Herzegovina on goal difference, despite Promise David's strike to cut the deficit.

entries.title: Vinícius Júnior silences critics with 2 more goals, giving him 4 in this World Cup for Brazil
entries.description: Brazil star Vinícius Júnior knew what his critics were saying coming into this World Cup, that he just can't score on soccer's biggest stage the way he can at Real Madrid.  Vinicius has four goals now in this tournament — only Lionel Messi, with five, has more — and the flashy Brazilian got two on Wednesday night in his team's 3-0 win over Scotland.  “Faith that I was going to improve, for the talent that I have … I was sure that at the right moment I would shine again with the Brazilian team shirt,” Vinícius said.

entries.title: Venezuela capital hit by double earthquake as experts warn of high death toll
entries.description: Delcy Rodríguez declares state of emergency after buildings collapse across Caracas, with tremors felt in ColombiaVenezuela earthquake – latest updatesVenezuela’s interim leader, Delcy Rodríguez, has declared a state of emergency after the country was struck by two powerful earthquakes that collapsed dozens of buildings and killed at least 32 people, with experts predicting the death toll could rise significantly.Rodríguez said hundreds were injured and more casualties were expected. The two strong earthquakes hit within a minute of each other. The first had a magnitude of 7.2 and the second 7.5, making it the most powerful to strike the country since 1900, according to the US Geological Survey (USGS). Continue reading...

entries.title: Datacentres facing increase in global climate-related legal cases, report finds
entries.description: LSE analysis highlights litigation linked to energy sources, water consumption and air pollutionThe proliferation of datacentres and AI is increasingly at the forefront of environmental litigation around the world from Chile to Ireland, a report has found.In an analysis of about 3,600 climate-related lawsuits filed since 2015, the latest annual review of climate litigation by the London School of Economics (LSE) found a growing number of cases challenging the energy sources, water consumption and air pollution of datacentres, all of which have related climate implications. Continue reading...
